messages.content: edit: This funded research project started on July 2022, it has made significant progress so far because of its great im-portance, it has led to the inevitable development of its scope, in a way that has led to five articles in the field of digital health and digital transformation in order to answer unanswered questions in the field of digital health. Now they are in the development stage and are expected to continue until the end of August 2024.

Edit: This funded research project commenced in July 2022 and has already achieved substantial milestones due to its paramount significance. The project's scope has naturally expanded, necessitating the creation of five scholarly articles pertaining to digital health and digital transformation. These articles aim to address previously unexplored queries within the realm of digital health. Currently, the articles are undergoing the development phase, with the expected completion projected for August 2024.

Revised Edit: This funded research project was initiated in July 2022, and it has made remarkable strides due to its exceptional importance. As a result, its scope has expanded, leading to the creation of five scholarly articles focused on digital health and digital transformation. These articles aim to shed light on unanswered questions in the field of digital health. Currently, the articles are in the development stage and are anticipated to be finalized by the end of August 2024.

edit: The new German Digital Healthcare Act (Digitale-Versorgung-Gesetz), which incentivizes statutory insured pa-tients through prescription and reimbursement to use certain digital health apps for the long-term treatment of their (e.g., psychological) health condition, has catapulted Germany into the league of front-runners in digital health policy (Gerke et al. 2020). However, it is largely unknown (a) whether such an incentive will actually have a significant impact on patients’ attitudes towards the use of digital health apps and (b) whether such incentives would also have an effect in other cultural contexts where patients are less long-term oriented concerning their health. These knowledge barriers obstruct the evaluation of the German policy’s effects and its possible transfer-ability to other national contexts.

Edit: The recent implementation of the German Digital Healthcare Act (Digitale-Versorgung-Gesetz) has positioned Germany as a leading advocate for digital health policy. The act incentivizes individuals with statutory health insurance to utilize specific digital health apps for the ongoing management of their health conditions, including psychological ailments (Gerke et al., 2020). Nevertheless, two critical questions remain unanswered. Firstly, it is unclear whether these incentives will genuinely influence patients' attitudes towards adopting digital health apps. Secondly, it is uncertain whether these incentives would yield similar results in other cultural contexts where individuals may prioritize short-term health goals. These knowledge gaps hinder the evaluation of the German policy's effectiveness and its potential transferability to other nations.

sumarize: The research project described in this proposal aims to address these knowledge gaps through a cross-country study in Germany and Iran. Starting from the Theory of Reasoned Action (Fishbein and Ajzen 1975) and situated in the literature on mobile health, we hypothesize that the intention to use a mobile health app is influenced by subjective norm of a doctor’s prescription and the patient’s long-term orientation, next to other variables known from the literature. In the course of a research stay of Dr. Jorfi in Hagen, we plan to design an online experiment with a sample of subjects from Germany (a country with high long-term orientation) and Iran (a country with low long-term orientation) to test our hypotheses. The results are expected to deliver key insights on the applicability of German policy regarding digital health in other socio-cultural contexts such as in the Middle East. The de-scribed project is also expected to lay a foundation for the development of a larger funding proposal, besides con-tributing to a more international work environment at our Chair and strengthening the visibility of the University of Hagen abroad.

Summary: This research project aims to bridge knowledge gaps by conducting a cross-country study in Germany and Iran. Grounded in the Theory of Reasoned Action (Fishbein and Ajzen, 1975) and existing mobile health literature, the project hypothesizes that the intention to use a mobile health app is influenced by factors such as subjective norm of a doctor's prescription and the patient's long-term orientation. The research team, facilitated by Dr. Jorfi's research visit in Hagen, plans to design an online experiment involving participants from Germany (with high long-term orientation) and Iran (with low long-term orientation) to test these hypotheses. The anticipated results will provide crucial insights on the feasibility of applying German digital health policies in culturally diverse contexts, particularly the Middle East. Additionally, the project aims to lay the groundwork for a larger funding proposal, foster an international work environment at the Chair, and enhance the University of Hagen's visibility globally.

sumarize: This research project aims to bridge knowledge gaps by conducting a cross-country study in Germany and Iran. Grounded in the Theory of Reasoned Action and existing mobile health literature, the project hypothesizes that the intention to use a mobile health app is influenced by factors such as subjective norm of a doctor’s prescription, the patient’s long-term orientation, social Influence, mobile health self-efficacy, and privacy concern. The re-search team, facilitated by Dr. Jorfi’s research visit in Hagen, plans to design an online vignette experiment in-volving participants from Germany (with high long-term orientation) and Iran (with low long-term orientation) to test these hypotheses. The anticipated results will provide crucial insights on the feasibility of applying German digital health policies in culturally diverse contexts, particularly the Middle East. Additionally, the project aims to lay the groundwork for a larger funding proposal, foster an international work environment at the Chair, and enhance the University of Hagen’s visibility globally.

Summary: This research project aims to fill knowledge gaps by conducting a cross-country study in Germany and Iran. Building upon the Theory of Reasoned Action and existing mobile health literature, the project proposes that the intention to use a mobile health app is influenced by several factors, including subjective norm, long-term orientation, social influence, mobile health self-efficacy, and privacy concern. With Dr. Jorfi's research visit in Hagen facilitating the project, an online vignette experiment will be designed, involving participants from Germany and Iran to test these hypotheses. The study's results are expected to provide valuable insights into the feasibility of implementing German digital health policies in culturally diverse contexts, especially in the Middle East. Moreover, the project aims to lay the groundwork for a larger funding proposal, promote an international work environment at the Chair, and enhance the global visibility of the University of Hagen.

sumarize: The recent implementation of the German Digital Healthcare Act (Digitale-Versorgung-Gesetz) has positioned Germany as a leading advocate for digital health policy. The act incentivizes individuals with statutory health insurance to utilize specific digital health apps for the ongoing management of their health conditions, including psychological ailments (Gerke et al., 2020). Nevertheless, two critical questions remain unanswered. Firstly, it is unclear whether these incentives will genuinely influence patients’ attitudes towards adopting digital health apps. Secondly, it is uncertain whether these incentives would yield similar results in other cultural contexts where individuals may prioritize short-term health goals. These knowledge gaps hinder the evaluation of the German policy’s effectiveness and its potential transferability to other nations.
This research project aims to bridge knowledge gaps by conducting a cross-country study in Germany and Iran. Grounded in the Theory of Reasoned Action and existing mobile health literature, the project hypothesizes that the intention to use a mobile health app is influenced by factors such as subjective norm of a doctor’s prescription, the patient’s long-term orientation, social Influence, mobile health self-efficacy, and privacy concern. The re-search team, facilitated by Dr. Jorfi’s research visit in Hagen, plans to design an online vignette experiment in-volving participants from Germany (with high long-term orientation) and Iran (with low long-term orientation) to test these hypotheses. The anticipated results will provide crucial insights on the feasibility of applying German digital health policies in culturally diverse contexts, particularly the Middle East. Additionally, the project aims to lay the groundwork for a larger funding proposal, foster an international work environment at the Chair, and enhance the University of Hagen’s visibility globally.
This funded research project commenced in July 2022 and has already achieved substantial milestones due to its paramount significance. As a result, its scope has expanded, leading to the creation of five research papers fo-cused on digital therapeutics (DTx). These articles aim to shed light on unanswered questions in the field of digital health adoption. Currently, the papers are in the development stage and are anticipated to be finalized by the end of August 2024.

Summary: The German Digital Healthcare Act has positioned Germany as a leader in digital health policy. However, uncertainties remain regarding the impact of incentives on patients' attitudes towards digital health apps and their transferability to different cultural contexts. To address these knowledge gaps, a research project will be conducted in Germany and Iran, examining factors that influence the intention to use mobile health apps. The project aims to provide insights on the applicability of German digital health policies in diverse contexts. Additionally, it aims to lay the groundwork for a larger funding proposal, foster an international work environment, and enhance the University of Hagen's global visibility. The project, which commenced in July 2022, has already made significant progress, resulting in the development of five research papers on digital therapeutics. The papers are expected to be completed by August 2024.

edit: Germany has gained recognition as a leading country in releasing a statutory mechanism-Digital Healthcare Act (Digitale-Versorgung-Gesetz)- in December 2019 for prescription and reimbursement of certain digital health apps listed in a new directory-DiGA (Digitale Gesundheitsanwendungen Verzeichnis) for insured patients (Gerke et al. 2020; Polin et al. 2021). A patients’ intention to use a health app is influenced by multiple behavioral, socio-demographic, and technology-related antecedents (Aljohani and Chandran, 2021; Zhaoa et al. 2018). However, it is largely unknown whether regulatory incentives (such as Germany’s new policy) and the subjective norm ex-pressed in a doctor recommending such an app will actually have a significant effect on patients’ intention to use a health app that is distinctive from the known antecedents. We therefore pose RQ1: Do prescription and reim-bursement of mobile health apps effectively influence patients’ intention to use the apps?
In addition, most digital health apps require continued usage over a longer time to have a positive effect on a patient’s health, while studies have shown that most patients abandon health apps already after relatively few weeks and only a few uses (Vaghefi and Tulu 2019). It therefore stands to reason that a patient’s long-term orien-tation, defined as the fostering of virtues oriented towards future rewards (Hofstede 1998), is a crucial factor in explaining the intention to use mobile health apps. A lack of understanding the influence of long-term orienta-tion, however, will obstruct the transferability of learnings from Germany’s new Act to other national contexts where patients are potentially less long-term oriented concerning their health. This motivates RQ2: How is the influence of a prescription and reimbursement policy on the intention to use potentially different in socio-cultural contexts with different levels of long-term orientation?

Edit: In December 2019, Germany introduced the Digital Healthcare Act (Digitale-Versorgung-Gesetz), which established a regulatory framework for the prescription and reimbursement of specific digital health apps listed in the Digital Health Applications Directory (DiGA) for insured patients. This initiative has positioned Germany as a frontrunner in digital health policy (Gerke et al., 2020; Polin et al., 2021). Previous research has identified various factors that influence patients' intention to use health apps, including behavioral, socio-demographic, and technology-related aspects (Aljohani and Chandran, 2021; Zhaoa et al., 2018). However, it remains uncertain whether regulatory incentives, such as the new German policy, and the subjective norm conveyed through doctor recommendations, have a significant impact on patients' intention to use health apps beyond these known factors. This study aims to investigate Research Question 1: Do the prescription and reimbursement of mobile health apps effectively influence patients' intention to use these apps? Additionally, many digital health apps require long-term usage for positive health outcomes, yet studies have shown that most patients discontinue using health apps after a short period with only a few instances (Vaghefi and Tulu, 2019). It is thus essential to understand the influence of patients' long-term orientation, defined as their focus on future rewards, on the intention to use mobile health apps. Without this understanding, the transferability of insights from Germany's Digital Healthcare Act to other countries, where patients may have different levels of long-term orientation concerning their health, would be limited. This leads to Research Question 2: How does the influence of a prescription and reimbursement policy on intention to use differ in socio-cultural contexts with varying levels of long-term orientation?

sumarize: To address our two research questions, we plan to design and conduct an online vignette experiment (Reips, 2002; Dandurand et al. 2008) and recruit approximately 1000 participants from Germany and Iran. Our analysis will take its outset from the Theory of Reasoned Action-TRA (Fishbein and Ajzen’s 1975) due to its applicability to understand an individual’s technology adoption (Otieno et al. 2016). TRA suggests two main antecedents, name-ly social-normative and personal-attitudinal factors (Vallerand et al. 1991; Zhang, 2013). In our context, the sub-jective norm refers to the extent to which the app is recommended by a doctor (with a prescription or not), or by another source such as a friend. Long-term orientation is a personal factor that varies across different cultural contexts (e.g., Germany and Iran). 
Hofstede (1998) reports that Germany with high long-term orientation has a significant cultural distance to Iran’s low level in this dimension of the model. We will use a between-subject design that confronts subjects from Ger-many and Iran with different scenarios as to the strengths of subject the subjective norm, which may influence their intention to use a health app, thus addressing RQ1. The intention to use will be measured equally for all groups by using validated scales from the technology acceptance literature (Hoque, 2016; Otieno et al. 2016; Vallerand et al. 1991). RQ2 will be addressed both by testing the direct effect of long-term orientation on the intention to use across the groups, as well as through a multigroup analysis by testing the effect of the subjective norm on intention to use across the German and Iranian subsamples.

Summary: To address the research questions, an online vignette experiment will be designed and conducted, involving approximately 1000 participants from Germany and Iran. The analysis will be based on the Theory of Reasoned Action (TRA), which is suitable for understanding technology adoption. TRA suggests that subjective norm and personal attitude are the main antecedents influencing intention to use. In this study, subjective norm refers to the influence of doctor recommendations or recommendations from other sources, while long-term orientation represents a personal factor that differs across cultural contexts. Germany, with high long-term orientation, is culturally distinct from Iran, which has a lower long-term orientation. A between-subject design will be utilized, presenting participants from both countries with different scenarios to assess the impact of subjective norm on intention to use a health app, addressing Research Question 1. Intention to use will be measured using validated scales from technology acceptance literature. Research Question 2 will be explored through analyzing the direct effect of long-term orientation on intention to use in both groups and conducting a multigroup analysis to examine the effect of subjective norm on intention to use among German and Iranian subgroups.

sumarize: First and foremost, given the importance of the identified knowledge gaps and the proposed research to address these gaps through the described project, we expect a solid contribution in the mobile health area leading to a joint publication at a reputable journal in Information Systems. The methodological expertise and relevant do-main knowledge of the two researchers will greatly contribute to this expected outcome. 
Second, the applicants regard this project as a basis for developing a larger funding proposal in the area of digital health directed towards a funding organization such as the German Research Foundation (DFG) and/or the Ger-many Ministry of Health (BMG). This follow-up project will employ survey and interview methods for a multi-stakeholder evaluation of the German DVG policy, involving primary physicians and real patients. 
Third, we expect this research project to help initiate a long-term partnership between the Iranian universities that the guest researcher works with and the University of Hagen, specifically the Chair of Information Manage-ment at the Faculty of Business Administration and Economics. As a developing country, Iran has a growing need for scientific partnerships with top universities such as those in Germany. 
Fourth, the presence of international guest faculty on the campus in Hagen will greatly contribute to a more in-ternational work environment at the Chair of Information Management and the Faculty of Business Administra-tion and Economics. The guest will bring in experience from his many years in teaching, research and industry, which should benefit PhD researchers and faculty alike.
Fifth and lastly, this joint research project is expected to contribute to the international visibility of University of Hagen. Not only will the planned publication support the recognition of University of Hagen as a place where cross-national research has a home, but the host is also determined to promote the project with its international guest in the different internal and external communication channels of the University of Hagen.

Summary: This research project anticipates several outcomes and benefits. Firstly, the project aims to make a solid contribution to the field of mobile health, leading to a joint publication in a reputable Information Systems journal. The methodological expertise and domain knowledge of the researchers will contribute significantly to this outcome. Secondly, the project intends to serve as a foundation for a larger funding proposal focused on digital health, targeting funding organizations such as the German Research Foundation and the German Ministry of Health. This subsequent project will involve survey and interview methods to evaluate the German Digital Healthcare Act with various stakeholders, including primary physicians and patients. Thirdly, the project seeks to initiate a long-term partnership between Iranian universities and the University of Hagen, fostering scientific collaborations between the two countries. The growing need for such partnerships in Iran presents an opportunity for collaboration with top German universities. Fourthly, the presence of the international guest researcher at the University of Hagen will contribute to a more international work environment at the Chair of Information Management and the Faculty of Business Administration and Economics. The guest's experience in teaching, research, and industry will benefit both PhD researchers and faculty members. Lastly, this joint research project is expected to enhance the international visibility of the University of Hagen. The planned publication and promotion of the project through various communication channels will highlight the university as a hub for cross-national research.
